scenario: A FEEL expression normalises customer names: convert to lowercase, then capitalise first letter. The team wonders about FEEL string functions.
question: Which FEEL string built-ins handle case conversion?

options.text: Use toUpperCase() and toLowerCase() for case conversion in FEEL -- these are Java and JavaScript method names using camelCase dot notation that does not apply to FEEL's function invocation style. FEEL uses space-separated function names without object-method syntax. The correct FEEL built-ins are upper case(s) and lower case(s). Using toUpperCase() in FEEL would result in an evaluation error.

Use the capitalize(s) built-in to convert the first letter to uppercase -- this function name is invented and has no entry in FEEL's string built-in function catalogue. Camunda's FEEL does not include a single-built-in title-case or capitalise function. To capitalise the first letter and lowercase the rest, teams must compose: =upper case(substring(s, 1, 1)) + lower case(substring(s, 2)).

upper case(s) (all uppercase), lower case(s) (all lowercase) are the FEEL built-ins for case conversion. Note the space-separated naming convention consistent with other FEEL functions. For "Title Case" / capitalise (first letter uppercase, rest lowercase) FEEL has no single built-in -- compose using: =upper case(substring(s, 1, 1)) + lower case(substring(s, 2)). For word-boundary title case, use split, for-in-return, and string join. Alternatively use replace with regex for more complex patterns.

FEEL includes no case conversion functions and string values are always returned as-is -- this misrepresents the string built-in capabilities. md and are available in Camunda 8's FEEL engine. Case conversion is commonly used for case-insensitive comparisons (lower case(a) = lower case(b)) and for normalising user input before storing it in process variables.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL string built-ins for case: upper case(s): returns string converted to uppercase. Note FEEL's space-separated name. lower case(s): returns string converted to lowercase. For more complex case manipulations: Title case (capitalise first letter of each word): not a single built-in; compose with substring, upper case, lower case, and possibly split + string join + map operations. First-letter-upper-rest-lower: =upper case(substring(s, 1, 1)) + lower case(substring(s, 2)) (assuming s is non-empty). Example for full title case (using Camunda's canonical for-in-return projection per feel-list-expressions.md:88-148, NOT bracket-comprehension which is Pattern #23 fabrication): ` =string join( for w in split(s, " ") return upper case(substring(w, 1, 1)) + lower case(substring(w, 2)), " " ) Anti-pattern (avoid): =[w in split(s, " ") : upper case(...) + lower case(...)] // Pattern #23 -- fabricated bracket-comprehension Camunda FEEL's projection syntax is for x in list return expr (feel-list-expressions.md:88-148); its filter syntax is list[predicate] with implicit item (:67-86). The DMN-spec [binding in list : predicate] form does NOT compose with Camunda FEEL. Use cases: Normalisation for comparison: lower case(a) = lower case(b)` for case-insensitive match. Display formatting: convert user input to standard case for display.
